nativeRange.habitat: The Black Cap Basslet inhabits near-vertical drop-offs and walls beyond the outer reef, occurring at depths of roughly 10–180 m and most commonly encountered between 20 and 60 m. Specimens are often observed swimming upside-down beneath overhangs and retreating into crevices when threatened.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a variety of meaty marine fare in captivity — mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine fish and crustacean flesh, and quality frozen, pellet, or flake preparations. In the wild, has been reported feeding on zooplankton and small crustaceans (copepods, isopods, amphipods). Specimens tend to do best with several small feedings per day.

avoidWith: very large or predatory fish (lionfish, moray eels, groupers, snappers)

imageCandidates.notes: Hectonichus; 1838x1380. Whole fish in frame, vivid magenta body with dark purple/black cap along the head and nape; head in sharp focus, vibrant in-colour specimen.
Aquaimages (English Wikipedia); 600x400. Textbook Black Cap Basslet showing the distinct jet-black cap over a bright purple body; lower resolution so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: A deep-reef Caribbean basslet with a vivid purple body and jet-black cap, generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. Best kept singly; tends to be territorial toward other basslets.
careNotes:
The Black Cap Basslet is generally considered one of the more straightforward saltwater fish to keep and is often recommended for reef aquariums. In the wild it inhabits near-vertical walls and drop-offs at moderately deep reef sites, so specimens tend to appreciate well-aquascaped rockwork with caves, overhangs, and crevices, and have been reported to acclimate better under slightly subdued lighting.

Conspecific and inter-basslet aggression is the main behavioral concern: best kept as a single specimen in most tanks. Pairs and small groups have been attempted in larger systems but typically need substantial footprint and multiple discrete territories in the rockwork. Generally advised not to house with Royal Grammas, other basslets, or similar-shaped dottybacks.

Like other Grammatidae, has been reported to be a notable jumper — a tightly fitted lid or mesh top is generally advised. Specimens tend to be shy when first introduced and may take time to venture into open water; they often orient belly toward the substrate and have been observed swimming upside-down under ledges, which is normal behavior for the family.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but is uncommon at the hobbyist level. Reared juveniles do appear in the trade (sold as 'captive-bred' or 'tank-raised'), so commercial-scale rearing is feasible. Like Royal Gramma, males are believed to establish nest sites in small holes and crevices and guard eggs until hatching; detailed protocols for the hobby aquarium remain sparse.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive rearing is documented at commercial scale (tank-raised specimens appear in the trade) but home-aquarium breeding is uncommon and detailed protocols are sparse. Pairing is challenging due to conspecific aggression.
